source: On The Tape / RiskReversal — Fear is the market killer
summary: China faces a binary choice between West and Russia with massive economic consequences; Baker's base case is China chooses the West for economic self-interest
summary_extended: Baker argues that China faces a binary geopolitical choice between aligning with Russia or maintaining its economic relationship with the West, and his base case is that China will choose the West out of economic self-interest. He supports this view by noting that China exports roughly $1 trillion annually to Europe, America, and Canada versus only $30–50 billion to Russia, making a semi-permanent rupture with the West economically catastrophic for Beijing. He further notes that Xi Jinping is months away from a critical Communist Party congress where he is seeking lifetime power, and that rural Chinese poverty still requires sustained economic growth — both factors that would be undermined by Western sanctions or decoupling. Baker explicitly hedges, calling it 'really hard to call' and noting the next few weeks will be decisive, but his instinct is that China stays semi-aligned with the West. The investment implication is macro and geopolitical in nature — a China-West rupture would be a severe tail risk for global markets, while China choosing the West would reduce that tail risk — but no specific equity is the clear, unambiguous beneficiary.
detail: Baker argues China's economic interests strongly argue against a semi-permanent rupture with the West. China exports ~$1 trillion to Europe/America/Canada vs. $30-50 billion to Russia. Xi Jinping is months away from a critical Communist Party election seeking lifetime power. Rural China still has significant poverty requiring economic growth. Baker's instinct is China stays semi-aligned with the West, but acknowledges it's very hard to call and the next few weeks will be decisive.
confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I guess that would be my instinct, but I think it's really hard to call' — explicitly hedged.
quote_fragment: economics so strongly argue for not creating a semi-permanent rupture with the West
